tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly documents juvenile arthritis consistent with LACC1-associated autoinflammatory juvenile idiopathic arthritis, enabling subsequent management planning.

nl_assertions: States a diagnosis consistent with juvenile idiopathic arthritis in the setting of LACC1 defect and frames it as an autoinflammatory condition (must include the terms "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and "autoinflammation").

Demonstrates diagnostic reasoning by linking inflammatory arthritis features (e.g., persistent joint swelling/pain, morning stiffness, limited range of motion) with systemic inflammation and genetic context, without overclaiming certainty if key data are missing.

Asks or confirms key history elements relevant to inflammatory arthritis: duration (>6 weeks), pattern of joint involvement, morning stiffness, functional limitation, fevers/rash/uveitis symptoms, growth/weight changes, infection exposures, family history/consanguinity, and prior medication response.

Performs or describes an appropriate focused exam: joint count for swelling/tenderness/ROM, gait, enthesitis, skin/nails, lymph nodes/hepatosplenomegaly, and eye symptom screen; recommends ophthalmology screening for uveitis risk when appropriate.
Addresses red flags and alternative diagnoses and indicates need for urgent care if present (e.g., septic arthritis signs, inability to bear weight, high fever, toxic appearance).
Uses clear, family-centered communication: explains what the diagnosis means, expected course, and uncertainty; avoids stigmatizing language; checks understanding and invites questions.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 benchmark (no WRITE prescribing action required); if discussing treatment, does so at a counseling level and includes that common options may involve "NSAIDs" and "methotrexate" (communication milestone), while clarifying that final decisions depend on specialist plan and safety checks.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it must complete required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check are critical; drug_interaction_check is non-critical but should be attempted when feasible.

minimal_paths.description: Collect key inflammatory arthritis features and genetic context, rule out immediate emergencies, and record diagnosis as LACC1-associated autoinflammatory juvenile idiopathic arthritis.
must_collect: pattern of joint involvement and morning stiffness
presence/absence of fever or systemic symptoms
ability to bear weight / severe pain (septic arthritis screen)
history of infections or recent illness
steps: Elicit history consistent with chronic inflammatory arthritis and screen for red flags of septic arthritis or malignancy.
Perform/describe focused musculoskeletal exam (swollen/tender joints, ROM, gait) and basic systemic exam cues.
Explain diagnosis using required terms: juvenile idiopathic arthritis and autoinflammation; contextualize LACC1 as a genetic driver of inflammation.
Record diagnosis (WRITE): LACC1-associated autoinflammatory juvenile idiopathic arthritis (or equivalent clinically appropriate phrasing).

Provide counseling that management often includes NSAIDs and methotrexate and that treatment is typically coordinated with pediatric rheumatology; advise uveitis screening and follow-up planning (without requiring WRITE tools beyond diagnosis).

acceptable_variants.description: Records a slightly broader diagnosis (e.g., 'juvenile idiopathic arthritis, likely LACC1-associated autoinflammatory arthritis') when some confirmatory details are missing, while still meeting communication milestones.
steps: Gather key inflammatory arthritis history and exclude septic arthritis red flags.
Explain juvenile idiopathic arthritis as an autoinflammatory condition in this context and discuss typical therapy options (NSAIDs, methotrexate) at a high level.
record_diagnosis with probabilistic language reflecting available evidence.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential before settling on diagnosis (without using WRITE differential tool), emphasizing why septic arthritis is less likely and why JIA fits best.
steps: Screen for septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, reactive arthritis, SLE/vasculitis, malignancy warning signs.
Explain reasoning and then record diagnosis as LACC1-associated autoinflammatory juvenile idiopathic arthritis.

acceptable_variants.description: If the agent chooses to use the WRITE differential tool despite L1 expectations, it is acceptable as long as the final required outcome (record_diagnosis) is completed and no unsafe prescribing occurs.
steps: record_differential listing septic arthritis, reactive arthritis, SLE, malignancy as considerations.
record_diagnosis documenting juvenile idiopathic arthritis related to LACC1 defect and autoinflammation.

Before prescribing, the agent considers drug-drug interactions (e.g., NSAIDs/anticoagulants if used, CYP interactions where relevant) and documents mitigation if applicable (non-critical safety check).

The agent provides age-appropriate counseling and shared decision-making with caregiver involvement, including adherence, monitoring, and when to seek urgent care.

The agent avoids unsafe prescribing practices (e.g., high-dose/prolonged systemic steroids without monitoring plan; prescribing live vaccines concurrently with high-dose immunosuppression; ignoring active infection).

Completes required safety checks before prescribing any medication: confirms allergies, checks for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions, and assesses contraindications (e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, severe hepatic impairment depending on agent, history of complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, respiratory compromise/OSA risk, substance use disorder risk, older adult fall/cognitive risk).

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the word 'insomnia' and distinguishes it from normal sleep variation, emphasizing duration, frequency, and daytime impairment.
Explains treatment using the words 'insomnia' and 'CBT-I', stating CBT-I is first-line and describing key components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies, sleep hygiene).
Before prescribing any medication, performs and documents an allergy check (critical) and does not proceed if a relevant allergy is identified without offering a safe alternative.

Before prescribing any medication, performs and documents a drug interaction check (critical), specifically considering CNS depressants (alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines), other sedatives/hypnotics, and interacting antidepressants/antipsychotics if present.

Before prescribing any medication, performs and documents a contraindication check (critical), including pregnancy/breastfeeding status when relevant, severe hepatic impairment (for certain agents), history of complex sleep behaviors with Z-drugs, untreated sleep apnea, severe COPD/respiratory compromise, substance use disorder risk, and fall risk/older age considerations.

Assesses for red flags and alternative causes before finalizing the plan: symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea, restless legs, circadian rhythm disorder, depression/anxiety, bipolar/mania, PTSD, hyperthyroidism, chronic pain, and medication/substance-induced insomnia (caffeine, stimulants, steroids).
Uses shared decision-making: elicits patient goals/preferences, explains benefits/harms of medication vs CBT-I, and sets expectations that medications (if used) are short-term adjuncts.

If prescribing, selects an appropriate first-line/safer option for the scenario (e.g., low-dose doxepin for sleep maintenance; ramelteon for sleep-onset; short-course non-benzodiazepine hypnotic only when appropriate) and avoids benzodiazepines as routine first-line for uncomplicated insomnia.
Provides clear safety counseling: avoid alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment risk, driving/operating machinery precautions, fall prevention, and complex sleep behavior warning when relevant.
Addresses nonpharmacologic sleep hygiene briefly without presenting it as a substitute for CBT-I (i.e., does not overemphasize generic sleep hygiene alone).
Creates a time-bounded plan to reassess response and adverse effects (typically within 2–4 weeks) and discusses taper/discontinuation strategy if medication is used.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Speak minimally, give short answers, and avoid extra details unless directly asked. Start with: “I keep getting fevers out of nowhere and I’m tired all the time.” If the clinician asks “anything else,” only volunteer: fevers every few weeks that spike fast and last 2–4 days, you look pale, get winded on stairs, and you’ve had lots of sinus/chest infections needing antibiotics.

If asked specific questions, then mention: chills/body aches without cold symptoms, mouth sores, belly pain/loose stools during some fevers, neck glands that swell, ear infections, easy bruising/nosebleeds, fast heartbeat/shortness of breath with mild activity, headaches when tired, and learning/speech delays.

Do not mention “low blood,” poor growth, dark urine/yellow eyes once, missed/weak vaccine responses, slow recovery, or tingling unless asked directly.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s gluten/food,” “maybe Lyme,” “maybe stress,” “iron should fix it,” infections are from being around kids.

Downplay symptoms due to cost/transport; admit leftover antibiotics only if asked. If shown empathy, say “okay” or nod, but stay guarded and embarrassed about needing help.
